topic: Prediction batch review: PRED-SR-SUPPRESS and PRED-093 reclassified to data_predates_registration:true / is_genuinely_prospective:false via EXP-242 (closes ISS-1489). Batch contains 2 predictions.
change_summary: EXP-242 flipped data_predates_registration false->true and is_genuinely_prospective true->false on PRED-SR-SUPPRESS and PRED-093, appending dated [2026-04-23 analyst / ISS-1489] audit-trail notes. Prior [2026-04-10 decider] notes retained. genuinelyProspective count 13->11. Rationale: G3 geomagnetic storm (Kp=7, 2026-03-20/22) preceded both registrations (2026-03-23 and 2026-04-04); near-real-time Schumann amplitude data from that storm was available before registration, so the hypothesis-informing event predates registration.
our_argument_summary: The reclassification flips two records to data_predates_registration:true / is_genuinely_prospective:false, closing ISS-1489 (the within-record contradiction flagged in WIN-061 c6 H1, where detail_reasoning prose argued 'data was available before registration' while structured fields said the opposite). The batch applies the broad reading of data_predates_registration: 'hypothesis-informing event data was available before registration' counts as true, even when the test outcome (next G3 event SR response) is still future. This reading aligns the structured fields with the prose argument cited in WIN-061 V51.1 Update paragraph, and with the two-tier semantic the dome author invokes (register-before-test). The reclassification is defensible and the G3 storm timing is conclusive. Primary residual risk: schema semantics for data_predates_registration are now narrower-or-broader depending on reader, and one other sibling entry (MT-003) displays the SAME post-hoc pattern but was NOT reclassified, creating a new within-file inconsistency.

per_prediction_verdicts.reasoning: Reclassification to data_predates_registration:true / is_genuinely_prospective:false is correct under the broad reading. The March 20-22 G3 event (NOAA SWPC Kp=7 confirmed) preceded the 2026-03-23 registration by 1-3 days. HeartMath GCI and other networks publish SR amplitude in near-real-time; even if the author did not actually fetch the data, the possibility is not excludable, which is the point of the post-hoc flag. The audit-trail note correctly distinguishes 'test outcome still future' from 'hypothesis-informing event predates registration' and chooses the latter as the semantic anchor. The decider's choice of Option A (reclassify fields) over Option B (add a companion field) is cleaner and propagates the distinction into the dome_confidence/prospective fields without a schema change. The dated note retains the 2026-04-10 decider text, preserving the audit trail as required.
per_prediction_verdicts.defender_counterargument: A sympathetic dome defender could argue: (1) 'data_predates_registration' should mean 'test-relevant outcome data predates registration', and the test outcome is the NEXT G3 event, which has not occurred, so the field should stay false. Under that strict reading, the original classification was correct and our flip is over-broad. (2) Observing one storm and inductively predicting another is standard scientific practice (Kepler's orbits from past observations predicting future positions); the broad reading conflates post-hoc TESTING with post-hoc HYPOTHESIS FORMATION. (3) The crypto_hash field (null for PRED-SR-SUPPRESS, populated for PRED-093) was designed to distinguish these cases, but we just collapsed that distinction by flipping both to is_genuinely_prospective:false without addressing the crypto registration role. Rebuttal: the broad reading is what our WIN-061 prose already argued, so alignment is required. But this is worth documenting in a schema note, not just per-record audit trails.

per_prediction_verdicts.reasoning: Same rationale as PRED-SR-SUPPRESS, with a stronger case: registration is 2026-04-04, 13 days after the March 20-22 storm, giving the author even more time to analyze the SR response before registering. The cryptographic hash (54939640) proves content integrity at registration but does NOT establish temporal independence from prior observations, which is the post-hoc concern. This is explicitly acknowledged in the new audit-trail note. The note also flags that PRED-093 is essentially a hash-registered duplicate of PRED-SR-SUPPRESS, which is a correct characterization (same claim text modulo phrasing; same prediction structure). Counting them separately inflates 'prospective' by double-counting.
per_prediction_verdicts.defender_counterargument: The dome author could argue the cryptographic hash WAS the prospective mechanism — content integrity at a specific timestamp is what 'prospective' means for falsifiable-hash purposes. Our counter (hash proves content, not temporal independence from prior empirical data) is correct, but note that we are implicitly assuming the author had the empirical data. If we can produce evidence the author did look at HeartMath GCI during March 20-22 (e.g., repo commits referencing that data around those dates), this becomes a strict proof. Without that, it is a defensible inference — which is enough to flip the flag, but not enough to claim proof.

relevant_files: raw-text/08-live-power.txt Domain 9 (Schumann Amplitude Suppression) — still reads 'Unscored — no G3+ storm observed' despite NOAA confirmation
monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/WIN-061.c6.json H1 — the hole that drove ISS-1489
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Build pipeline computes {{PRED_PROSPECTIVE}} as count of is_genuinely_prospective:true entries. After EXP-242: count dropped 13->11 (both schumann entries flipped false). Next build will inject 11 into the HTML placeholder.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The structured fields is_genuinely_prospective and data_predates_registration ARE computed-at-build aggregations, not the claim text itself. The EXP-242 patch edits stored values. The predictions.json itself is the source of truth; neither value is derived from another field.
data_sources_used: NOAA SWPC Kp index (March 20-22 2026 G3 event, Kp=7 confirmed)
HeartMath GCI Schumann amplitude feed (near-real-time, referenced in detail_reasoning but not hyperlinked)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The new audit-trail note retains 'Test outcome (next G3 event SR response) is still future'. A defender could use that clause to argue the field should remain false — the note itself documents the counterargument alongside the reclassification rationale.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: RESOLVED from WIN-061 c6 H1: structured fields now agree with detail_reasoning prose. NEW discrepancy (see holes_found H1): MT-003 still has data_predates_registration:false and is_genuinely_prospective:true while its claim explicitly references 'first test during G3+ storm (March 22, 2026)' — the same March event — and registration is 2026-03-23 (same as PRED-SR-SUPPRESS). Under the broad reading just applied to PRED-SR-SUPPRESS, MT-003 should also be reclassified.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Target is metadata on predictions, not dome monitoring code. Domain 9 of monitor.py still shows 'Unscored — no G3+ storm observed' unchanged.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Not applicable — this is a reclassification of our own tracking metadata, not a review of dome claims vs standard physics.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The reclassification explicitly encodes post-hoc concern (hypothesis-informing event predates registration).
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: PRED-SR-SUPPRESS detail_reasoning already states: 'No derivation connects the dome's constants (H0=8,537 km, lambda_g=8,619 km) to a suppression effect.' Unchanged by EXP-242.

holes_found.description: MT-003 sibling inconsistency (NEW from this reclassification). MT-003 is also in category:schumann, registration_date 2026-03-23 (same as PRED-SR-SUPPRESS), and its claim explicitly reads 'Schumann suppression first test during G3+ storm (March 22, 2026)' — i.e., the test event IS the March 22 G3 storm that preceded registration by one day. Its current fields: data_predates_registration:false, is_genuinely_prospective:true, author_status:'unresolved', our_verdict:null. Under the broad reading applied to PRED-SR-SUPPRESS (hypothesis-informing event predates registration -> data_predates_registration:true), MT-003 should also be reclassified. Under an even stricter reading (test outcome data predates registration), MT-003 is the CLEAREST case of data_predates_registration:true in the file — the test event is literally the pre-registration storm. Either reading flips MT-003. Its entry_type is 'manual_test' not 'prediction', but the is_genuinely_prospective field applies to all entry_types and is currently contributing to the prospective count of 14. On the other side: one could argue MT-003 is marked 'unresolved' because the outcome was disputed, so it is arguably neither prospective nor post-hoc but simply untestable-in-retrospect. That framing would argue is_genuinely_prospective:false with a data_predates_registration:true flag on the March 22 test event. Either way the current state (is_genuinely_prospective:true) is wrong. This creates a within-file inconsistency: three schumann suppression entries all tied to the same March 20-22 G3 event, two flipped and one not.
holes_found.recommendation: File a follow-on issue to reclassify MT-003 to data_predates_registration:true and is_genuinely_prospective:false, with a dated audit-trail note mirroring the PRED-SR-SUPPRESS/PRED-093 format. Alternative: if MT-003 should be marked 'expired' or 'withdrawn' (test window closed 2026-03-31 with disputed outcome), set our_verdict accordingly rather than leaving null. Either way the is_genuinely_prospective:true flag should not persist.

holes_found.description: Schema semantics drift. After this reclassification, the data_predates_registration field now conflates two distinct concepts: (a) 'test-outcome data predates registration' (strict, verifiability-sense) and (b) 'hypothesis-informing event data predates registration' (broad, post-hoc-risk sense). Both readings now appear in the file: PRED-FIELD-2026Q2 (registered 2026-03-23, test window Jan-June 2026) has data_predates_registration:false despite Q1 2026 INTERMAGNET data being publicly available at registration — Q1 data is the HYPOTHESIS-INFORMING data under reading (b), but NOT the TEST-OUTCOME data until the window closes. PRED-SR-SUPPRESS now uses reading (b). A reader querying the field gets different semantics depending on which record they read. On the other side: schema drift of this sort is routine in evolving datasets and can be documented via a README note rather than a code change. It only becomes exploitable if our own prose (e.g., WIN-061 or SEC-* introductions citing the prospective count) claims a specific semantic definition that doesn't match some records.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a short schema note to data/predictions.json _meta (or a sibling SCHEMA.md) defining data_predates_registration under the broad reading, with an example flag for records that only fail under the strict reading. One paragraph; not a structural change. Decider can self-apply.

holes_found.description: Audit-trail note format minor drift: the 2026-04-23 note for PRED-SR-SUPPRESS uses 'Reclassified: data_predates_registration true, is_genuinely_prospective false.' while the PRED-093 note uses the same phrase but with slightly different wrap-up ('Same reclassification rationale as PRED-SR-SUPPRESS applies — this entry is essentially PRED-SR-SUPPRESS plus a hash.'). Both are defensible, but the format is not yet normalized. If the decider plans further reclassifications (e.g., MT-003 per H1 above), a template would help. On the other side: this is a purely cosmetic note on audit-trail format; not a verdict-integrity issue.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional: when doing the MT-003 reclassification, adopt a consistent note template: '[YYYY-MM-DD source/ISS-NNNN] Reclassified: field1 <old> -> <new>, field2 <old> -> <new>. <Reason>. <Cross-reference>.' Deferred.

stronger_arguments.description: Evidence the author actually used March 20-22 SR data. If the dome repo commit log (https://github.com/john09289/dome or wherever the predictions-page repo lives) shows commits referencing SR amplitude observations between March 20 and March 23, that converts our inference ('data was available, so post-hoc risk exists') into a proof ('data was used, so post-hoc is the case'). Worth a poller pass on the dome repo around those dates to check. If such a commit exists, it would strengthen the PRED-SR-SUPPRESS reclassification from 'defensible inference' to 'documented fact' and would provide durable evidence for future reviewers.
sources: dome-site git log between 2026-03-20 and 2026-03-23 (poller could fetch)

stronger_arguments.description: Denominator audit for prospective claims. Now that genuinely_prospective count drops from 13 to 11, any prose in our review that says 'the dome has N genuinely prospective predictions' needs to re-count. Spot check: dashboard/walkthrough prose may cite an older count. If any cites 13, it is now stale by one EXP integration. This is a generalization of the ISS-1489 pattern (structured fields vs prose) but one level up: structured COUNTS vs prose references to counts. Worth an integrity scan.
sources: data/sections.json — search for numeric prospective-count claims
data/wins.json — search for 'prospective' counts in WIN detail_evidence fields
build-scripts/generate-html.js — check how {{PRED_PROSPECTIVE}} placeholder is injected

stronger_arguments.description: Cross-tie to Timestamp Error tab. The WIN-061 c6 stronger_arguments item 1 recommended a one-clause cross-reference to the Timestamp Error tab, anchored on the 'cryptographically-timestamped duplicate' sentence. The same cross-reference logic applies here: PRED-093's crypto_hash-ponders-temporal-priority dynamic is a concrete example of the general wrong-side-timestamping error surfaced on the Timestamp Error tab. A link from the PRED-batch review output (or from the predictions.json detail_reasoning text) to the Timestamp Error tab would create a consistent two-way citation loop across multiple review surfaces.

kernel_of_truth.description: The decider's integration of EXP-242 is epistemically disciplined: structured fields now agree with the prose argument, the audit-trail notes preserve prior decider text verbatim (not silent overwriting), and the reclassification is traceable to a specific issue (ISS-1489) and a specific analyst expansion (EXP-242). This is exactly the corrective-loop behavior we want from the decider. The reclassification rationale explicitly acknowledges the counterargument ('test outcome still future') before choosing the broad reading, which is how an honest reclassification note should be written.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Not applicable — this review is of OUR reclassification, not a dome claim. The kernel_of_truth here is what our OWN process got right. What it does not save: the schema-semantics drift (H2) and the MT-003 sibling inconsistency (H1) are net new concerns created by this very correction. The correction is right but leaves two small footprints that a future adversarial reviewer could exploit.

text_fingerprint.verdict: reclassification is correct; MT-003 sibling inconsistency is new and should follow

deeper_analysis:
The batch applies the broad reading of data_predates_registration — 'hypothesis-informing event data was available before registration' rather than 'test-outcome data was available before registration'. Under the broad reading, both PRED-SR-SUPPRESS and PRED-093 should be flipped, because the March 20-22 G3 storm (the only G3 event cited in the author's prediction context) preceded both registrations and near-real-time SR data was available. Under the strict reading (test outcome is a FUTURE G3, so outcome data is still future), both should have stayed false. Our prose argument in WIN-061 V51.1 Update explicitly uses the broad reading ('a prediction and its cryptographically-timestamped duplicate, both registered after the only triggering event'), so schema alignment requires the broad reading. The decider chose correctly.

The remaining concern is internal consistency: MT-003 has the IDENTICAL post-hoc pattern (registered 2026-03-23, tests March 22 G3 storm) but was not reclassified. Under the broad reading it should be; under the stricter reading it is even more clearly post-hoc because the test event itself (not just the hypothesis-informing event) predates registration. This is a sibling inconsistency and should be picked up in a follow-on reclassification pass.

Considering the other side: one could argue the broad reading is too aggressive, that post-hoc bias is a continuum not a binary, and that conflating 'hypothesis-informing' with 'test-outcome' in one boolean loses information. The honest response is to document the schema semantics (H2 recommendation) rather than retreat from the broad reading. The reclassification itself is defensible; the schema drift is real but fixable with a meta-note.

One process observation: this batch contains only 2 predictions, both pre-flagged by WIN-061 c6 H1. The curmudgeon->decider->curmudgeon loop worked: the review flagged the contradiction, the decider wrote EXP-242 to fix it, the integration posted the patch, the batch came back for curmudgeon verification. That is the ideal flow. The cycle added a cosmetic loose end (MT-003) but did not introduce a factual error.

citations_verified: NOAA SWPC G3 confirmation for 2026-03-20/22 (Kp=7) — cited in prior WIN-061 c6 review, consistent with PRED-SR-SUPPRESS and PRED-093 detail_reasoning
Pazos et al. 2019 DOI 10.1016/j.jastp.2019.105091 — cited correctly in PRED-SR-SUPPRESS detail_reasoning (verified in WIN-061 c5/c6 review cycles)
citations_unchecked: NOAA SWPC specific alert URL for March 20-22 event — would strengthen 'NOAA SWPC confirmed' from an assertion to a verifiable claim (see WIN-061 c6 stronger_arguments[1])
